data.title: Hang Seng Ends Lower, Still Up for the Week
data.description:
The Hang Seng dipped 223 points or 0.9% to close at 24,859 on Friday, reversing gains from the prior four sessions amid broad-based losses.

Sentiment soured ahead of the August 12 deadline for China to reach a lasting tariff deal with the Trump administration.

Traders also remained cautious ahead of China’s July CPI and PPI data due Saturday, with expectations for a slight drop in consumer prices amid weak demand and continued producer deflation due to lingering trade risks.

The tech sector led losses, down around 1.5%, pressured by a nearly 8% plunge in chipmaker SMIC after disappointing Q2 earnings.

Other notable decliners included Mixue Group (-3.1%), Trip.com (-2.3%), and Sands China (-2.3%).

Still, the index gained 1.4% for the week, partially recovering from last week’s 3.5% fall, helped by upbeat July trade data from China.

Meanwhile, mainland stock markets hovered near 10-month highs, buoyed by optimism around the upcoming NPC Standing Committee meeting and Q2 earnings season.

data.title: Steel Rebar Maintains Decline from 7-Month High
data.description:
Steel futures in China fell to CNY 3,210 per tonne from the seven-month high of CNY 3,345 on July 29th as markets reconsidered the scale of Chinese fiscal support against the slowdown in consumption and the prolonged property crisis.

Beijing said it would implement expansionary fiscal and monetary policy to shore up spending but refrained from signaling large-scale stimulus measures.

Still, the outlook of lower supply and robust infrastructure spending maintained steel futures 4% higher in the third quarter.

Chinese policymakers vowed to erase overcapacity in construction inputs, with Baosteel expecting domestic output to fall by over 50 million tonnes this year due to policy bounds.

Consumption also maintained its level in the near term as Chinese furnaces imported over 100 million tonnes of iron ore in July, a 1.8% annual gain, as steel exports soared by 11.4% annually year to date, the highest increase for the period on record, despite protectionist trade policies worldwide.

data.title: Pakistan suspends mobile data service in restive province
data.description:
Pakistan has suspended cell phone data services for three weeks in the restive southwestern province of Balochistan in a bid to block communications among separatist insurgents behind a surge in recent attacks, an official and the government said.

Separatist militants demanding a bigger share of profits from the resources of the mineral-rich province have stepped up attacks in recent months, particularly on Pakistan's military, which has launched an intelligence-based offensive against them.

In an order on Wednesday seen by Reuters, the government said the services would be suspended until the end of the month because of the law and order situation in the province, home to key Chinese Belt and Road projects.

"The service has been suspended because they (militants) use it for coordination and sharing information," Shahid Rind, a spokesperson for the provincial government, said on Friday.

Officials said there are 8.5 million cell phone subscribers in Balochistan, Pakistan's largest province by size, which borders Afghanistan and Iran. But it is thinly populated, with just 15 million from a national population of 240 million.

The news follows Pakistan's ban on road travel to Iran late last month, citing security threats.

The insurgency by the separatists, who accuse Pakistan's government of depriving them of their share in regional resources, has roiled the province for decades.

They primarily attack Pakistani military or Chinese nationals and their interests, but have recently started targeting senior army officers.

The military said an officer and two soldiers were killed in a roadside blast set off by the militants on Tuesday.

The attack targeting a vehicle was claimed by the Baloch Liberation Army (BLA), the strongest of the area's insurgent groups, which has also claimed responsibility for several attacks on senior officers in recent weeks.

The region is home to the Gwadar Port, built by Beijing as part of a $65-billion investment in Pakistan in the Belt and Road programme designed to expand China's global reach.

Islamabad accuses arch-rival India of funding and backing the insurgents in a bid to stoke instability, as Pakistan seeks international investments in the region, a charge New Delhi denies.

In March, the BLA blew up a railway track and took hostage more than 400 train passengers in an attack that killed 31, including 23 soldiers.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0424 GMT - LG Chem's share price could get a boost from a potential stake sale for stronger shareholder returns, Nomura analyst Cindy Park writes in a note. The company could dispose of part of its 81.8% stake in battery-making affiliate LG Energy Solution, the third-largest market-cap company in South Korea, and return a portion of the proceeds to shareholders, Park says. She is positive about LG Chem's efforts to streamline its businesses, citing Thursday's announcement to sell its aesthetic unit to a private equity firm for 200 billion won. Nomura raises its target price for LG Chem by 20% to 360,000 won and keeps a buy rating. Shares are 6.5% down at 273,500 won. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0323 GMT - Taiwan chip makers TSMC and GlobalWafers should be exempt from the U.S.'s 100% semiconductor tariff, given their U.S. operations and ongoing expansion plans, Goldman Sachs analysts say in a research note. This could position TSMC and GWC more favorably among major U.S. customers, offering better cost advantages, they say. Meanwhile, as investors have been concerned about tariff uncertainties for months, the announcement helps remove valuation overhangs, they add. TSMC's share price has underperformed other AI names such as SK Hynix and Nvidia, partly due to geopolitical uncertainties, they say. The 100% tariff could also make TSMC's mature node's pricing--previously considered to be at a premium compared to peers--more competitive, they add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0312 GMT - Kakao's earnings growth momentum appears weak despite its stronger-than-expected 2Q results, Nomura analysts Angela Hong and Won Kang write in a note. The South Korean mobile internet company's solid 2Q profit margin was largely driven by reduced marketing expenses and other cost savings, they say. Operating profit in its core advertising and commerce segments remained flat on quarter and fell 17% on year in 2Q, they note. Nomura raises the target price by 23% to 59,000 won to reflect the 2Q margin improvement and the 2H launch of OpenAI-powered services but maintains a neutral rating on the stock. Shares are 0.9% higher at 64,200 won. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0150 GMT - The proposed 100% U.S. tariff on chips will hurt the Malaysian tech sector, and uncertainty over tariffs could delay investment and expansion in Malaysia, especially for companies reliant on U.S. demand,CIMB Securities analyst Mohd Shanaz Noor Azam says in a note. That is despite short-term gains for the sector due to increased demand before the tariffs take effect, he says. Automated test equipment makers like Greatech Technology and Genetec Technology, with high U.S. exposure, are most at risk, he says. Outsourced semiconductor assembly and testing players such as Unisem (M) and Malaysian Pacific Industries are more insulated as they have fewer direct U.S. shipments, he adds. CIMB names Inari Amertron and Malaysian Pacific Industries as its top picks in the sector. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0142 GMT - A portion of Malaysia's semiconductor exports may be exempted from the proposed 100% U.S. tariff, say CIMB analysts Michelle Chia and Azri Azhar in a note. About 65% of the country's chip exports to the U.S. come from U.S. firms having operations in Malaysia, with some of the remaining 35% potentially qualifying due to their U.S. links, they say. If exemptions fall below 65%, each additional 10% chip exports affected could drag Malaysia's GDP by 0.29%. In the near term, U.S. may consider the complex supply chain, but over time, companies may shift production, affecting Malaysia's investment outlook, they caution. CIMB maintains its 2025 GDP growth forecast for Malaysia at 4.3%, and expects Bank Negara to maintain the policy rate at 2.75% this year. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1736 GMT - Airbnb's management points to encouraging travel demand trends heading into the current quarter, but Wedbush Securities says it remains cautious given industry data says otherwise. Nights booked on the platform were stable last quarter versus the previous quarter in North America, and slowed across its EMEA and Latin America segments, according to analysts Scott Devitt and Matthew Weiss. They are keeping a close eye on any changes in demand through the rest of the year, given the company's relative exposure to the U.S., where consumers seem to have pulled back on spending the most amid macroeconomic uncertainty, they say. About a third of Airbnb's nights booked are in the U.S. and Canada, they say, lowering their price target on the stock to $130 from $135. Airbnb slides 9.7%. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1652 GMT - Apple's $2.5 billion investment in Corning's Kentucky plant should put Apple on President Trump's good side, say Wedbush analysts. The investment in the glass plant, which will produce all of the glass for iPhone and Apple Watches, is part of Apple's $600 billion investment commitment to the U.S. and should help ease concerns about the company's "growth initiatives with Trump heading down the 'America First'/tariff path," the analysts say. Producing iPhones solely in the U.S. is still unrealistic, the analysts say, but moves toward U.S. manufacturing should draw Apple away from Trump's tariff ire. Corning is up 0.7% and Apple rises 2.7%.(nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1634 GMT - DoorDash's food flywheel keeps turning, Capital Markets analysts say. The analyst upped its price target for DoorDash to $325 from $295 after the food delivery service recently swung to a profit and posted a surge in revenue in its most recent quarter. The analysts said DoorDash is a long-term share gainer in delivery and omni-channel commerce, especially after pushing initiatives to improve the customer experience, expand merchants and reduce average delivery times. The company's monthly active users surged, with engagement from both new and old customers, the analysts say. Shares rise 4% to $268.62. (natalie.weger@wsj.com; @NatalieWeger)

1536 GMT - President Trump's 100% tariff on semiconductors appears intimidating, but it could have limited consequence, given the exception for companies that commit to manufacturing in the U.S., says Bank of America analyst Vivek Arya. Many of the largest semiconductor companies including TSMC, Samsung and Intel have already committed to ramping up manufacturing in the U.S. Other companies, including Advanced Energy Industries and Texas Instruments, could see an initial hit, but could easily shift more manufacturing to the U.S. "Semis vendors have already started adjusting for the new normal, and impact on profitability could likely be limited over time," Arya says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1050 GMT - Deutsche Telekom's overall results were solid, UBS analysts Polo Tang and Dhruva Kusa Shah write in a note. Despite a competitive German mobile market, the telecommunications company continues to outperform, they say. The group raised its full-year targets for adjusted Ebitda and free cash flow after leases, driven by strong operational results at T-Mobile U.S., they note. The U.S. bank reiterates its view that the group could see 5%-10% mid-term earnings per share and free cash flow upgrades, supported by lower taxes and completion of announced M&A in the U.S., as well as German fiscal stimulus. Shares are down 3.7% at 30.14 euros. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

1030 GMT - President Trump's 100% tariff on chip imports could have a severe impact on many Asian economies, HSBC economists say in a research note. Asia accounts for more than 75% of global value-added activities in chip fabrication and roughly up to 85% in assembly, testing, and packaging, they say. Despite the U.S.'s effort to reshore chip manufacturing, it would be "immensely difficult" to replicate Asia's dominance given its advantages in infrastructure, human capital expertise and other factors, the economists say. It's still unclear how much investment is needed for a company to avoid the 100% sectoral tariff, they say. While chip companies will likely announce more investments in the U.S., Asia remains indispensable to the global chip supply chain, they add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

1004 GMT - Deliveroo appears to be gaining momentum just as it prepares to the exit the public market, AJ Bell analyst Danni Hewson. The British food-delivery company raised its full-year guidance after a solid first-half performance. With its takeover by U.S. rival DoorDash expected to complete later this year, Deliveroo is showing encouraging signs-- not just in order growth, but more importantly in underlying earnings, margins and cash flow, she says. In May, San Francisco-based DoorDash agreed to acquire Deliveroo for around 2.9 billion pounds in a move to expand its global presence. Shares are down 0.1% at 177.30 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0756 GMT - Roche Holding might need a pickup in dealmaking to offset a hit from upcoming patent expirations to its earnings, analysts at Morgan Stanley say in a research note. The Swiss pharmaceutical giant's drug pipeline lacks momentum and this is likely to continue into next year, Morgan Stanley says. "From here, further pipeline setbacks could see a deterioration in investor sentiment, with rising concerns that Roche is 'running to stand still' without significant business development to offset the steady cadence of patent expiries," the analysts say. Consensus estimates on Roche's earnings over the 2027-29 could be cut by 3% to 6% as a result, according to Morgan Stanley. Shares fall 0.6%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0738 GMT - Roche Holding finds itself in a tough spot, with earnings growth likely to peak this year, lack of pipeline momentum and a pivot into obesity at risk from market uncertainty and competition, Morgan Stanley analysts say. "We believe consensus expectations may be too optimistic given potential branded and biosimilar competition to the base business, which will likely increase over 2027-2029," they write in a research note. Trial results the Swiss pharma giant is due to release in the fourth quarter seem high-risk and 2026 looks set to be another light year for its drug pipeline, the analysts say. Morgan Stanley cuts its recommendation on the stock to underweight from equalweight, and trims its target price to 245 Swiss francs from 286 francs. Shares fall 0.8% to 247.10 francs. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0724 GMT - European luxury companies could start to see a rebound after weak results, Deutsche Bank analysts write in a research note. The second-quarter earnings season didn't throw up many surprises, with most luxury brands seeing a sequential slowdown from the previous three months, the analysts say. Companies that are more exposed to tourism and to lower-income consumers faced greater challenges, they add. Despite current difficulties, Deutsche Bank expects a sequential sales improvement in the second half. "The investor view on the sector remains largely negative but there are early signs of this starting to change," DB says. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0615 GMT - Riverstone Holdings retains a bull in RHB Research which sees the gloves manufacturer in a "sweet spot." The company is benefiting from a robust pickup in global semiconductor sales in 2025, which is estimated to grow 15.4%, according to World Semiconductor Trade Statistics organization. Cleanroom gloves manufactured by companies such as Riverstone are essential in semiconductor chip manufacturing to prevent contamination and ensure product quality. However, RHB cuts its earnings forecasts for Riverstone by 13% for 2025, 12% for 2026, and 13% for 2027 after the company's 2Q results were below expectations. It trims the stock's target price to S$0.85 from S$0.95 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are 4.8% lower at S$0.69. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0532 GMT - Novo Nordisk's oral semaglutide weight-loss pill is likely to dominate the narrative after Eli Lilly's orforglipron pill data came out on the lower side of expectations, Intron Health analysts Naresh Chouhan and Dominic Rose write. Orforglipron data looks competitive, but Novo Nordisk's oral semaglutide could see upside, Intron Health says. On a placebo-adjusted basis, the orforglipron weight loss is only 1% worse than Novo's Wegovy shot, and slightly worse versus oral semaglutide. However, Intron Health thinks the obesity market is nowhere near as big as the market believes, Novo's Ozempic diabetes drug estimates are far too high and margin forecasts need to fall. It upgrades Novo Nordisk to hold from sell and retains its 300 Danish kroner target price. Shares closed at 308.85 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0441 GMT - A potential 150% to 250% U.S. tariff on pharmaceuticals would have a limited impact on China's drug industry, Morningstar analyst Yurou Zheng says in a note. The proposed tariff has dragged down shares of drugmakers and sector companies globally. But Zheng notes that most of the Chinese healthcare companies under Morningstar's coverage either focus on domestic sales or supply the U.S. through licensing deals with American pharmaceutical companies, or already manufacture onshore. Sinopharm Group is Morningstar's preferred pick, given it is China's largest drug distributor. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

1418 GMT - Eli Lilly showed its weight-loss treatments are still in high demand, but less-than-stellar results from a study on an experimental anti-obesity pill is what investors are focusing on. The drug maker saw 2Q results jump from a year earlier, prompting it to raise its outlook for the year. Boosting the performance were robust sales for Mounjaro and Zepbound, rising 68% to $5.2 billion and more than doubling to $3.38 billion, respectively. But a study of Eli Lilly's orforglipron, the experimental anti-obesity pill, showed it helped people lose up to about 12% of their body weight after more than a year of treatment, which fell short of analysts' predictions of between 13% and 15% or more. Eli Lilly tumbles 14%. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1224 GMT - Germany's Merck KGaA reported a messy set of second-quarter results, UBS analysts say in a research note. Currency headwinds and one-time charges in its electronics segment offset an encouraging recovery in life science and a solid performance in healthcare, they note. "The key driver of the miss was one-time items in the electronics division which overshadowed continued solid recovery in life science and a strong profit performance at healthcare," the analysts say. The German life science-and-chemical group adjusted its full-year guidance to accommodate those divisional trends, but also raised its expectations of a currency hit and included recent acquisitions and business sales. "Overall, [the group's 2025] targets are broadly unchanged, with the better performing divisions absorbing the drags," they add. Shares fall 0.4%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0913 GMT - European luxury companies delivered another sluggish quarter, which prompted worries among many investors around the long-term growth potential of the industry, UBS analysts write in a research note. The limited visibility on third-quarter trends, as well as currency headwinds, seem to have delayed the prospect of a returning earnings momentum after now almost two years of downgrades, the analysts say. UBS expects a sector recovery no sooner than in the second half of 2026. This is due to sector-specific challenges and macroeconomic and political issues, they say. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0737 GMT - DBS's growth should be sustainable in the medium term, Maybank IB's Thilan Wickramasinghe says in a note. The Singapore lender generated above-market-trend growth across all pillars in 2Q, which he expects to be supported by DBS's scale and strong management execution. DBS says it can raise base dividends while rolling out its capital returns program until 2027, which provides yield clarity, he adds. He expects DBS's early deployment of artificial intelligence and new verticals such as digital assets to further consolidate its lead over rivals. He raises his 2025-2027 earnings forecasts by 5%-11%, based on his more positive operational assumptions. Maybank IB lifts its rating to buy from hold and raises its target to S$56.15 from S$45.26. Shares are 1.4% higher at S$50.45. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0718 GMT - NN Group's first-half solvency 2 ratio of 208% will excite investors, KBW analyst Michele Ballatore writes. The Dutch insurance and asset-management group's solvency 2 result solidly beat expectations, he adds. In the absence of any material shocks in the second half of the year, an upgraded share buyback could be closer than expected, he adds. Elsewhere, NN Group's operating capital generation beat consensus views by 7%, driven by a better-than-expected performance in its NL Life division, he adds. Shares trade up 1% at 61.46 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0705 GMT - Munich Re's 5% cut to its revenue guidance for the year will lead to a 2% drop in consensus estimates, RBC Capital Markets says in a note. The German reinsurer now expects its insurance revenue to come in at 62 billion euros, against 64 billion euros previously due to a lower expected result in its reinsurance division on foreign exchange fluctuations. Elsewhere, Munich Re backed its 6 billion euro net profit guidance after it confirmed its prereleased second-quarter net profit beat. It flagged strong earnings momentum across all business segments, which has translated into high consensus expectations, analyst Mandeep Jagpal and associate Sarah Chong write. "The stock has been very strong coming into these results, up 8% over the last week, partly due to sector rotation, we believe," they add. Shares fall 6%. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0422 GMT - United Overseas Bank's share buybacks and dividend yield are likely to support its share price, says OCBC Investment Research's Carmen Lee in a note. She reckons UOB's dividend yield is still attractive at around 5%, based on a S$1.80-a-share dividend, given probably lower interest rates in the coming quarters. The group should be able to ride out any medium-term market uncertainties, she adds, citing factors such as UOB's Asean network, prudent management and healthy balance sheet. Nonetheless, with the 1H results below market expectations, OCBC Investment Research cuts its rating on the stock to hold from buy. It also lowers fair value estimate on UOB's stock to S$38.20 from S$41.50. Shares are down 0.1% at S$35.68. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0338 GMT - DBS's strong performance has likely been priced in, Morningstar's Michael Makdad says in a note. DBS continued to deliver higher returns than its peers, with an annualized return on equity of around 17% in the second quarter versus 13% for OCBC and UOB, he says. Singapore's largest lender has also proven more resilient in the mid-2025 rate drop. The group was able to capitalize more effectively on a surge of liquidity to Asian financial hubs in early-May amid tariff tensions, he adds. "While DBS's performance merits a premium, we see relatively greater value in its peers at current levels." Makdad maintains his earnings forecasts for DBS and S$48.00 fair value estimate on the stock. Shares gain 1.9% to S$50.68. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0217 GMT - DBS keeps its bull at RHB Research after the Singapore lender delivered solid 2Q earnings. DBS's hedges and balance sheet growth helped the bank post more resilient figures than its peers, RHB's Singapore Research team says in a note. The Singapore bank says its S$0.24-a-share increase in annual dividends should be sustainable for 2026, which reaffirms RHB's thesis for capital returns and dividend commitments. Nonetheless, the analysts reckon DBS will report softer 2H numbers due to seasonality and U.S. tariffs. RHB raises its target price for DBS to S$52.80 from S$47.00 to reflect lower cost of equity expectations after DBS's strong earnings, and maintains its buy rating. Shares are 2.1% higher at S$50.80. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0118 GMT - ASX's bears at UBS think that a regulatory inquiry into the market operator will lead to a recurring increase in its operating costs. ASX's warning of a A$30 million hit from the Australian Securities and Investment Commission's inquiry is seen by UBS analysts as implying a one-off 4% impact to fiscal 2026 EPS. However, they anticipate another A$20 million in fiscal 2027 and also see elevated cost growth the following year. Cost growth is likely to outstrip revenue growth in fiscal 2027 and fiscal 2028, they warn clients in a note. UBS cuts its target price 7.9% to A$63.65 and keeps a sell rating on the stock, which is up 0.9% at A$64.80. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2304 GMT - Heartland's high-returning reverse mortgage book appeals to Forsyth Barr. But it's balanced by other lending businesses that generate lower returns on equity and warrant a discount to net tangible assets. "With Heartland trading at 0.8x net tangible assets we believe a bearish outcome is largely priced into its other lending," analyst Benjamin Crozier says. For the stock to move higher, Heartland would need to allocate more capital to reverse mortgages, and maintain excess returns from this business. "While we are attracted to this segment, given increased competitive pressures in the Australian market we expect growth to moderate rather than accelerate," Forsyth Barr says. Heartland could also improve performance from its other lending, but there are several headwinds to this near term. Forsyth Barr retains a "neutral" call on Heartland. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1033 GMT - The cost of insuring Swiss banks' credit against default using credit default swaps is unchanged as the 39% tariffs on Swiss imports into the U.S. come into effect. Investors expect the Swiss government to reach a favorable trade deal with the U.S. government. "The strength seen on Thursday for stocks in the region does serve to highlight an optimism that efforts to renegotiate the tariff rate will soon bear fruit," Rostro's Joshua Mahony says in a note. The 5-year credit default swap spreads on Swiss bank UBS Group remain unchanged at 48 basis points, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0937 GMT - Raiffeisen Bank International has been delivering improving operating trends and profitability in the past 12 months, UBS says in a research note, raising its rating on the stock to buy from neutral. The Austrian lender's second-quarter results showed that net interest income held up better than expected, fee generation improved and that other income also seems higher, while loan loss provisions remained at muted levels, analysts Mate Nemes and Nicolas O'Sullivan write. Consensus expectations don't appropriately reflect the higher prospective return on tangible equity, they say. Additionally, Raiffeisen could benefit if its planned counter-claim against Russian investment firm Rasperia is successful, they add. UBS also lifts its target share price to 31 euros from 20.6 euros. Shares jump 11% to 27.56 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0908 GMT - DBS's increasing dividend payout should set it apart from peers, Citi's Yong Hong Tan says in a note. The Singapore bank's quarterly payout rises to S$0.75 a share, compared with S$0.54 a year ago. DBS has committed to dividend growth and has a visible dividend pathway over 2025-2027, he says. DBS is also continuing its plan to buy back S$3 billion in shares. Tan expects the lender to buy these shares back at a slower pace if the share price stays elevated. Citi raises its target price on DBS to S$56.50 from S$48.10 and maintains its buy rating. Shares are 1.8% higher at S$49.75 after touching an intraday high of S$50. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

2316 GMT [Dow Jones]--Origin Energy's earnings guidance for its Energy Markets business in FY 2026 is front of mind for Jarden ahead of the company's annual result Thursday. Analyst Nik Burns expects Origin to signal underlying Ebitda of A$1.602 billion in Energy Markets. That's 9% ahead of Jarden's A$1.372 billion forecast for FY 2025. Driving the on-year earnings improvement are higher natural-gas and electricity gross profits, a lower cost to serve and the start-up of Eraring Stage 1 battery, Jarden says. "We forecast A$1.1 billion capex in FY 2026, with focus on support for new gas peaker investment and any possibility of life extension at Eraring," Jarden says. "We also look for any indication of plans to list Octopus Energy's Kraken business on the Nasdaq." (david.winning@wsj.com)

2323 GMT - Amplitude Energy could be conservative again when setting expectations for its natural gas production, suggests Jarden. Amplitude has overcome problems at its Orbost Gas Processing Plant, which is now producing some 68 terajoules of gas per day. Analyst Nik Burns forecasts an improvement to some 74 TJ/day in FY 2026 for total production of 4.4 million barrels of oil equivalent. "We note that Amplitude set very conservative production guidance in FY25 (then upgraded twice), so may follow suit once more," Jarden says. Amplitude is due to report its FY 2025 result on Aug. 19. Jarden also hopes for updates to costs and schedule of Amplitude's East Coast Supply Project and the timing of increasing nameplate capacity of the Orbost plant. It retains a "buy" call on Amplitude. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1909 GMT - Crude oil futures fall for a sixth consecutive session in choppy trade amid uncertainty about the impact of U.S. tariffs, Russia sanctions, and output increases by OPEC+. Reports of positive talks between the U.S. and Russia on the Ukraine war and a possible meeting between Presidents Trump and Putin "increase the chances of a ceasefire, and perhaps even an eventual peace deal, though talks could remain tricky and the outcomes uncertain," Eric Lee of Citi Research says in a note. "Our base case forecast remains for Brent to head toward the low-$60s/barrel by end-year amid softening markets" even if talks are slow, India partially reduces purchases of Russian oil and China picks up more discounted barrels, he says. "U.S. appetite to enforce could remain low due to worries about high oil prices." Brent falls 0.7% to $66.43 a barrel and WTI settles down 0.7% at $63.88. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1320 GMT - Oil futures are modestly higher after a five-session losing streak driven by OPEC+ plans to raise output and concerns that U.S. tariffs, which take effect today for dozens of countries, will eventually crimp demand. Market views differ on whether India will or won't stop buying Russian crude, although the additional 25% U.S. tariff is still weeks away and Russia says Presidents Trump and Putin are expected to meet in coming days. Additional increases in OPEC production are the "overriding negative consideration," Ritterbusch says in a note. WTI is up 0.3% at $64.56 a barrel and Brent is 0.3% higher at $67.12. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1200 GMT - Canadian Natural Resources' 2Q performance was as straight down the fairway as could be hoped for, and it continues to deliver, Raymond James' Michael Barth says. As such he view the quarter as neutral, with production in line with his and the consensus forecast and a 3% beat on adjusted funds from operations. Raymond James has an outperform call on the shares, which were last at C$43.12, down 2.8% year-to-date. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0917 GMT - BP's fortunes are starting turn around after the energy giant delivered significantly stronger results along with positive news about a potential discovery in Brazil's Bumerangue field, Berenberg analysts write. They upgrade their rating on the stock to buy from hold and raise its target price to 500 pence from 385 pence. The oil major's outlook has been boosted by stronger free cash flow after it cut costs and lowered capital expenditure, they add. The incoming chairman will also review costs further while examining its portfolio. As net debt falls, BP should continue attractive buybacks in 2026, they add. Shares trade down 0.3% at 428.95 pence.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0808 GMT - U.K. and South African gold mining stocks rise in early morning trade after gold futures hit a record high on a report that the U.S. has placed tariffs on imports of one-kilo gold bars. In early trade, futures rose 1% to $3,489.50 a troy ounce after reaching a peak of $3,534.20 earlier in the session. In London, Hochschild Mining is up 3.1% and Fresnillo rises 2.4%. In Johannesburg, Sibanye-Stillwater rises 5.2% and Gold Fields is up 1.5%.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0424 GMT - LG Chem's share price could get a boost from a potential stake sale for stronger shareholder returns, Nomura analyst Cindy Park writes in a note. The company could dispose of part of its 81.8% stake in battery-making affiliate LG Energy Solution, the third-largest market-cap company in South Korea, and return a portion of the proceeds to shareholders, Park says. She is positive about LG Chem's efforts to streamline its businesses, citing Thursday's announcement to sell its aesthetic unit to a private equity firm for 200 billion won. Nomura raises its target price for LG Chem by 20% to 360,000 won and keeps a buy rating. Shares are 6.5% down at 273,500 won. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0158 GMT - Liontown is unlikely to generate positive cashflow until FY 2028, according to Citi analyst Kate McCutcheon. That is based on Citi's lithium-price forecasts, which average around US$830/metric ton through the end of next year. "While LTR is a quality orebody with expandable scale, we keep our sell rating on valuation grounds," she says in a note. Citi's target on Liontown is cut to A$0.40 from A$0.50 previously. Liontown shares are halted. The stock last traded at A$0.845/share. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2320 GMT - Jarden wonders whether Liontown's equity raising is big enough. The roughly A$266 million placement buys Liontown time to navigate depressed lithium prices, and dilutes the negative cash-flow-per-share and earnings-per-share metrics forecast for the next few years, analysts Ben Lyons and Adam Bennett say. Yet they estimate the SC6 spodumene price needs to average roughly US$1,100/metric ton in FY26 and US$1,000/ton in FY27 for Liontown to break even on free cash flow. So, the equity essentially provides a roughly 18-month buffer for spot prices to rise by about 50%, Lyons and Bennett say. Jarden downgrades the stock to "underweight" from "neutral." Its target price rises to A$0.50/share from A$0.48 before. Liontown last traded at A$0.845. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1833 GMT - Gold futures gain ground, with jobless claims rising more than expected this week and the Bank of England cutting rates for a fifth time this year. The developments have investors thinking a rate cut in the U.S. will come soon. "The weak data spurred markets to increase bets that the Fed will cut rates at its September meeting and beyond," says ADM Investor Services in a note. Front-month gold futures rise 0.6% to $3,400.30 a troy ounce. That makes it four out of the past five sessions that gold finished higher.(kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1423 GMT - The agriculture economy remains mixed, Corteva executives say. Low corn and soybean prices are hurting farmer profits, but growers are still shelling out for top-tier crop seed and pesticide products from Corteva to try and maximize their harvests. The company posts increased sales in its seed and pesticide sales in North America for its latest quarter. To boost farmer's fortunes, Corteva CEO Chuck Magro says increased China buying, new trade routes and more crops being used in biofuels are needed. "The market is certainly expecting a record harvest in the U.S.," Magro says on an analyst call. "We're getting close to harvest here in North America and opening up global markets to allow for trade of these critically needed crops would help American farmers continue to feed the world." (patrick.thomas@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1437 GMT - Hertz Global's fleet rotation has left it with more than 80% of its U.S. vehicles being less than a year old, CEO Gil West says on a call with analysts. The rental-car company has moved aggressively to swap out the fleet and realign it to better reflect consumer preferences, he says. As a result, depreciation per unit in 2Q hit $251, exceeding Hertz's goal of being below $300. Hertz took early actions to get pre-tariff pricing for its model year 2025 vehicles while selling older vehicles into a frothy market for used cars, the CEO says. The company now has a smaller but higher quality fleet, which should provide for better unit economics going forward, he says. "This approach proved effective, yielding strong cash proceeds and positioning us for more efficient growth," West says. Hertz Global rises 11%.(dean.seal@wsj.com)

1032 GMT - Rheinmetall held back from lifting its medium-term guidance on Thursday but it is still set to raise it once the German government provides some clarity around its defense budget in September or October, mwb research Jens-Peter Rieck tells Dow Jones Newswires. The German arms maker reported a softer second quarter, but its underlying growth story remains clearly intact, he says. The stock's pullback is a good entry point for investors, especially considering the record backlog, the potential for a very strong second half, and the drivers for increased long-term demand, he adds. Free cash flow turned sharply negative, reflecting high capex and inventory build-up ahead of an expected second-half acceleration, Rieck says. Shares are down 5% at 1,694.50 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0945 GMT - The trade deal between the European Union and the U.S hasn't yet brought any clarity or improvement for the German automotive industry, Hildegard Mueller, president of the German association of the automotive industry, says in a comment. The sectoral tariffs of 27.5% on passenger cars and automotive parts, which have been in force since April and May, respectively, remain in place and are a considerable burden on German car manufacturers and automotive suppliers as well as transatlantic trade, Mueller says. She calls on the U.S. to withdraw the sectoral automotive tariffs. It is important that the relief measures are implemented promptly, she adds. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0821 GMT - Cathay Pacific's near-term profitability looks weak, Lorraine Tan, director at Morningstar, writes in a note. Inflationary pressures on staff, passenger and route expenses outweighed savings from lower fuel prices and aircraft charges in 1H, and these cost pressures are expected to persist in the near term, Tan says. Yield normalization was faster than expected, causing a sell-off after the results, and the share price still looks overvalued, Tan says. Morningstar lowers its 2025 estimates for passenger and cargo yields but raises its passenger capacity and load factor forecasts. Morningstar trims its fair value estimate by 9% to HK$9.70. Shares closed 3.0% lower at HK$10.52. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

data.title: SUMMARY FROM THE EXTRAORDINARY GENERAL MEETING OF SOLTECH ENERGY SWEDEN AB (PUBL)
data.description:
Soltech Energy Sweden AB (publ) (”Soltech” or the ”Company”) held an extraordinary general meeting today, 8 August 2025, in Stockholm whereby the following resolutions were passed by the meeting.

Directed issue of consideration shares

The meeting resolved, in accordance with the board of directors’ proposal, to increase the Company’s share capital through a directed share issue of not more than 56,691,168 shares. The shares are issued as payment to Artim Balance BidCo AB (a company controlled by Nordic Capital) in connection with Soltech’s acquisition of all shares in the solar energy company Sesol Group AB. The resolution was unanimous and was made in accordance with the majority requirement set out by the Swedish Securities Council as a condition for the validity of the exemption from the mandatory bid obligation granted to Artim Balance BidCo AB in relation to the subscription of the consideration shares.

Determination of number of members of the board of directors, election of two new members of the board of directors, and remuneration to newly elected members of the board of directors

The meeting resolved, in accordance with the board of directors’ proposal, for the period until the end of the next annual general meeting, that the board of directors shall be expanded from four to six members, that Jacob Langhard and Thomas Mejdell shall be elected as new members of the board of directors, and that a yearly remuneration of SEK 300,000, together with remuneration for any committee work in accordance with the decision of the most recent annual general meeting, shall be paid to each of the newly elected members of the board of directors, and that such remuneration to these members for the period from the time upon which their election takes effect until the end of the next annual general meeting shall be paid in proportion to the length of their respective terms. The resolutions are conditional upon and takes effect from the time of the Company’s completion of the acquisition of all shares in Sesol Group AB.

Amendment of the articles of association

The meeting resolved, in accordance with the board of directors’ proposal, to amend the number of shares in the Company’s articles of association from being not less than 80,000,000 and not more than 320,000,000 to being not less than 185,000,000 and not more than 740,000,000, and to amend the share capital in the articles of association from being not less than SEK 4,000,000 and not more than SEK 16,000,000 to being not less than SEK 9,250,000 and not more than SEK 37,000,000. The resolution is conditional upon that all new shares issued in the consideration share issue resolved on by the meeting are allocated and registered with the Swedish Companies Registration Oﬃce.

Authorisation for the board of directors to issue shares, warrants and/or convertibles

The meeting resolved, in accordance with the board of directors’ proposal, to authorise the board of directors to, for the period until the next annual general meeting, on one or more occasions resolve on issue of shares, warrants and/or convertibles, with or without deviation from the shareholders’ preferential rights, to be paid in cash, in kind and/or by way of set-off, whereby the number of shares that may be added though new subscription or conversion may not result in the number of shares exceeding the number permitted by the articles of association, and that the authorisation from the annual general meeting 2025 shall cease to be effective. The resolution is conditional upon that the new articles of association adopted by the meeting are registered with the Swedish Companies Registration Oﬃce.

For further information on the proposals that have now been approved by the extraordinary general meeting, please refer to the information in the notice to the extraordinary general meeting published on 9 July 2025.

data.title: Novo continues rally on Lilly's disappointing drug data
data.description:
** Shares of Novo Nordisk rise 4% in morning trade to nearly top of STOXX with analysts saying it extends gains after disappointing Eli Lilly's Orforglipron data

** Novo shares surged about 7% on Thursday after Lilly's data showed its oral weight-loss drug candidate helped patients lose 12.4% of their body weight compared to 14.9% weight loss offered by Novo's injectable treatment Wegovy

** Nordnet analyst Per Hansen notes that besides showing less weight loss than anticipated, Lilly's pill had a less convincing side-effect profile, challenging prior market assumptions of its superiority

** The market reaction suggests some investors may have been caught on the wrong side of their bets, Hansen says

** "It seemed like an 'easy win' to be 'long' LLY and short Novo Nordisk ahead of LLY's earnings report," he adds

** Broker ABG believes that some investors are beginning to question whether they need to reverse the Lilly/Novo (and other obesity stocks) trade

** "The share price development over the past year has been so significant that even the 21% relative movement of Novo compared to Lilly pales in comparison to the overall one-year performance," ABG's analyst Morten Larsen says

** Novo shares suffered significant losses last week and earlier this week, a total of -35%, triggered by profit warning, CEO change announcement, and negative reactions to the company's Q2 report

** Shares of Novo's smaller rival Zealand Pharma rise 2%, after being up 4% on Thursday

data.title: Bulgaria Construction Output Growth Picks Up in June
data.description:
Construction output in Bulgaria increased by 4.3% year-on-year in June 2025, following a downwardly revised 3.6% rise in the previous month, which had marked the weakest growth since September 2024.

The improvement was driven by faster growth in building construction (4.2% vs 2.8% in May) and specialized construction activities (3.6% vs 0.3%).

In contrast, growth in civil engineering works slowed sharply (5.5% vs 9.6%).

On a seasonally adjusted monthly basis, construction output edged down 0.2% in June, after a 1.6% decline in May.

data.title: Bulgaria Consumer Mood Worsens in Q3
data.description:
Bulgaria’s consumer confidence dropped to -21.1 in the third quarter of 2025 from -16.4 in the previous three-month period.

This marked the lowest reading since the fourth quarter of 2023, as consumers turned more pessimistic regarding the financial situation for their households (-16.8 vs -7.7 in Q2) and the economic situation of Bulgaria for the next 12 months (-31.6 vs -21).

At the same time, households were less likely to make major purchases for durable goods in the present (-25.7 vs -31.8) and current assessments on saving propensity were more negative (-33.4 vs -31.4).

Lastly, concerns for the next twelve months were heightened about inflation (33.6 vs 18.8), while lesser for unemployment (15 vs 19.5).

data.title: UK's Pennant International slides after forecasting lower H1 revenue
data.description:
** British logistics software provider Pennant International Group's shares fall ~14% to 24p

** Co expects FY turnover to miss market expectations due to delays in GenFly contract awarded by UK Ministry of Defence in August

** GenFly contract award delayed to Q4 2025 or later due to MOD approval processes, pushing revenue into 2026, PNIG says

** Co expects H1 revenue of 4.5 million pounds, down 39.2% from prior year

** Including session's gains, PNIG down 11.6% YTD

data.title: Qube Celebrates 20 Years of Business Events Impact with Industry Recognition and Vision for the Future
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR, Aug 8, 2025 - (ACN Newswire) - Qube Integrated Malaysia Sdn. Bhd. (Qube), a leading local business events industry player celebrating its 20th anniversary this year, won 10 prestigious awards at the Malaysia Business Events Awards (MBEA) 2025, organised by the Malaysian Association of Convention and Exhibition Organisers and Suppliers (MACEOS) last night.

The company, an established name in the MICE (Meetings, Incentives, Conferences and Exhibitions) industry, was honoured at the event held at the Kuala Lumpur Convention Centre with five Excellence Awards and five Merit Awards, out of the 10 categories it was shortlisted for, including:

1. Exhibition Management Excellence Award: Qube for Malaysia International Halal Showcase (MIHAS) 2024

2. Trade Exhibition Excellence Award for events between 10,001-20,000 sqm: BuildXpo Malaysia 2024

3. Booth Design Excellence Award for booths below 72sqm held in overseas: MyCEB Pavilion at IMEX Frankfurt

4. Booth Design Excellence Award for single deck booth 73 sqm & above: Proton Pavilion at KLIMS 2024

5. Stage Design Excellence Award: Acer APAC Predator League 2025

6. Trade Exhibition Excellence Merit Award (20,001 sqm and above): MIHAS 2024

7. Event Excellence (B2B - Corporate) Merit Award: Proton e.MAS 7 Grand Launch

8. Booth Design Excellence Merit Award (Double Deck): Malaysia Airlines @ MATTA Fair - March 2024

9. Merit Award Booth Design Excellence Merit Award (Double Deck): Malaysia Airlines @ MATTA Fair - Sept 2024

10. Purpose-Built Venue Excellence Merit Award (15,001 sqm and above): Malaysia International Trade and Exhibition Centre (MITEC)

Founded in 2005 and headquartered in Kuala Lumpur, Qube marks its 20th anniversary this year with a bold step forward in digital innovation, talent development, and regional collaboration. Reinforcing its long-term commitment to shaping the future of MICE, the company has announced several new initiatives that underline its role as a strategic player in Malaysia's trade and tourism growth.

Over the last two decades, Qube has grown from a boutique design-and-build agency into a powerhouse for creative and executional excellence in exhibitions and events. The company has delivered Malaysia's pavilions at global showcases, including Expo 2017 Astana and Expo 2020 Dubai, which drew millions of visitors. In 2024, Qube set a significant milestone when the company acquired the tenure to manage the Malaysia International Trade and Exhibition Centre (MITEC), the country's largest business events venue.

In 2024, Qube also acquired SEA Healthcare & Pharma Show (SEACare), a long-running business trade exhibition in the medical and pharma sector. Most recently, Qube designed, built and managed the Malaysia Pavilion at the 2025 Mobile World Congress in Barcelona, and continues to manage MIHAS, the world's largest halal trade fair and a Guinness World Records title holder.

On this achievement, Richard Teo, Executive Chairman of Qube, said: "This recognition means a great deal to us. It reflects the dedication of our team, the trust of our clients, and the strength of the partnerships we have built. These platforms do more than showcase what we can do. They position Malaysian brands globally, create new opportunities for SMEs, and attract long-term business and investment into the country."

"Business events are not just about putting people in the same room. They help drive innovation, stimulate economic activity, support jobs and strengthen supply chains. At the heart of it all, we are focused on creating meaningful impact for our clients, our people and the nation," he added.

Collaboration with the Public Sector

Qube continues to work closely with public agencies including MATRADE, the Construction Industry Development Board (CIDB), the Malaysia Convention & Exhibition Bureau (MyCEB) and others. These partnerships align with national priorities under the 13th Malaysia Plan and ongoing tourism recovery efforts, particularly in SME development, export growth, talent upskilling and business event-driven tourism.

Looking ahead, Qube will play a central role in several major upcoming platforms:

- MIHAS 2025, in partnership with MATRADE

- SME Venture@ASEAN 2025, with SME Corporation Malaysia

- ICW & BuildXpo 2025, co-organised with CIDB Malaysia

In addition, Qube will host its signature event, WE2025: Future in Motion, a flagship initiative for empowering women in business, leadership, and innovation, from 25 to 27 November 2025. Themed Women in Trade. Investment. Leadership., this multi-platform event is organised in collaboration with the Ministry of Women, Family and Community Development and the Department of Women Development.

WE2025 features four integrated pillars: a leadership conference, a large-scale trade and investment expo, curated business matching and key side events.

Aimed at women entrepreneurs, corporate leaders, SMEs, startups, youth, ecosystem enablers and inclusive industries from across ASEAN and beyond, WE2025: Future in Motion is expected to attract 10,000 visitors, 1,000 delegates, 500 exhibitor booths, and generate RM100 million in trade and investment opportunities.

"We have always believed in using our platforms to make a lasting difference. Whether it is through delivering a high-impact trade show, empowering local brands, or raising the bar in venue management, our purpose has remained the same. Our growth is powered by talent, shaped by innovation, and guided by the values of inclusivity and excellence," added Teo.

----

Adnoc Gas Posts Record Earnings Despite Weaker Prices

The Abu Dhabi-owned gas company said net profit for the quarter rose 16% on the same period a year prior, setting a fresh quarterly record.

----

Siemens Energy Expects Results at Upper-End of Guidance After Record Order Intake

The German maker of energy equipment reaffirmed its guidance after swinging to a net profit.

----

Saudi Aramco Keeps Dividend After Profit Takes Hit From Lower Oil Prices

Aramco pumped more oil but lower prices hurt its top line as crude on average fell to $66.7 a barrel from $76.3 earlier this year.

----

BP Pledges Further Cost, Portfolio Review

The London-based energy company said it would launch a new cost review and evaluate its portfolio, as it presses ahead with a plan to address investor concerns.

----

BP Makes Potentially Largest Discovery in 25 Years Offshore Brazil

The energy giant said it discovered oil and gas in the deepwater Santos basin offshore Brazil as it seeks to grow global upstream production output.

----

Residential Solar Industry Looks to Cut Costs as End to Tax Credits Looms

With President Trump's tax law putting an early end to solar tax credits, the residential solar industry is now looking to lower prices, particularly by cutting sky-high sales and marketing costs, according to companies and analysts.

data.title: U.S.'s Timeline to Strike EU Trade Deal Unrealistic, TDI Chief Says — Market Talk
data.description: The timeline U.S. President Trump's administration imposed on EU negotiators to strike a trade deal was invariably unrealistic, says Jay Truesdale, a former U.S. diplomat and CEO of Washington D.C.-based intelligence firm TDI. This is given the scope of the topics and the complexity required to address important details. Both sides reached a political agreement in late July imposing a baseline 15% tariff on most EU goods. However, finer details such as what precise goods would be exempted from that rate and an anticipated joint statement that outlines the main points of the trade deal are still being finalized. (edith.hancock@wsj.com)

data.title: Oil Set for Sharp Weekly Loss on Trump-Putin Meeting News, U.S. Tariffs — Market Talk
data.description: Oil prices are on track for sharp weekly losses on news that President Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin could meet as soon as next week and as U.S. tariffs weigh on the global economic outlook. In early trade, Brent crude and WTI are up 0.1% to $66.50 and $63.90 a barrel, respectively, but are down 4.6% and 7.7% on the week. A meeting to discuss a Russia-Ukraine peace deal would significantly reduce the geopolitical risk premium and possibly affect Trump's secondary tariffs on India, easing market anxiety over supplies. Meanwhile, fresh U.S. tariffs on major trade partners and weak U.S. economic data are fueling concerns over demand just as OPEC+ moves to fully unwind its largest tranche of voluntary output cuts. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: Gold Futures Hit Record High After Tariff Report
data.description:
By Giulia Petroni

Gold climbed to a fresh record high on Friday following a report that the U.S. slapped tariffs on imports of one-kilo gold bars, a move that threatens to disrupt the global bullion market.

In early trade, futures rose 1% to $3,489.50 a troy ounce after reaching a peak of $3,534.20 earlier in the session.

The tariffs on one-kilo gold bars--the most common form traded on Comex--deal a fresh blow to top refining hub Switzerland, the Financial Times reported, citing a letter from Customs Border Protection.

Gold's rally is also driven by concerns over the global economic outlook after President Trump's new tariffs came into effect Thursday and weak U.S. data spurred bets that the Federal Reserve will soon cut interest rates.

According to the CME Group's FedWatch tool, traders currently see a 89.4% probability that the Fed will cut its benchmark interest rate by 25 basis points in September.

Meanwhile, Trump nominated Stephen Miran to fill a vacancy on the Fed's Board of Governors on a short-term basis. "Miran is the architect of Trump's tariff policy, with the market viewing the appointment as a tilt to a more dovish monetary policy," analysts at ANZ said.

Write to Giulia Petroni at giulia.petroni@wsj.com
